feedback.text: Ladies and Gentlemen, in an increasingly connected world such as our own, the Digital Fairness Act makes the necessary changes to the law to place the power of the people back into their own hands. Curbing unfair digital market practices is an important change that needs to be implemented in an increasingly online world. I've had personal experiences regarding the topic, from online marketplaces that hide information important to the consumer in sneaky ways, to subscriptions whose pricing is written in the most confusing way possible. I think almost every single person who has interacted with a device that is connected to the internet has in some way interacted with a system that is unfair or malicious to them specifically. I'd like to add another article to the supposed "Unfair practices" which is very near and dear to my heart, it pertains to entertainment products, video games specifically. With innovation in the videogame market, games have become increasingly ephemeral and transitional, ever-changing. These games receive online updates to their framework almost every single day, from new content to bug fixes. Developers and publishers spend huge sums of money and manpower to endlessly support and expand these titles, to something akin to a service, yet sell them as products at the same time. What is supposed to happen then, when these services stop generating profit? What happens when these games stop being supported? There are multiple avenues for developers to take currently, but the most efficient one and the one most publishers seem to take, either due to budgetary concerns or its ease, is to shut the game down on short notice and then stop the servers on which it runs. This, as one might be able to guess, has catastrophic results for the players of said game most of the time. From online services not functioning anymore, to even being entirely unable to start the product one paid money for. It isn't just short-sighted, it is straight up anti-consumer. Deep in the depths of End-User Licence Agreements, one will find wordy and hard-to-understand explanations about how the product one "bought" is actually just a license to a service they are allowed access to. These do not serve any other purpose than to throw the legal responsibilities of the publisher and developers off their own backs, leaving customers without a product they actually bought. I've personally experienced it with games I bought with my own money. Burnout Paradise, a racing game I bought for the Xbox 360 when I was a young boy as my first ever videogame. Its online services were taken offline entirely in 2018, and users who wished to continue playing the game online were forced to buy its remastered version, released for new generation gaming consoles. Customers could count themselves lucky that the only part of their game that was destroyed was the online mode of the game, leaving the single-player mode entirely functional and intact. Yet, not every video game is that lucky. The Crew, another open-world racing game I played, very similar to Burnout Paradise, had its servers shut down in March of 2024. Due to the game's online-only nature, this left the game entirely unplayable, leaving eager players to sit on its title screen with an error message to greet them. There are hundreds of games already released and thousands more in development, which the same scenario could or has already befallen, leaving EU citizens who bought it out of their money AND the product which they bought, simply due to the whims of the publisher or developer. The Digital Fairness Act is the legislation that could change this behavior by developers and publishers. Which is why I urge you, dear Commission, to please add an article to the Digital Fairness Act to add protections that protect customers from having their favourite videogames made inaccessible or destroyed by the publisher or developers after they have purchased them. I hope my pleas reached you.
